title: Phase 0 - Fundamentals
goal: Establish the rule that disciplined discovery, specification, testing, and verification happen before confident implementation claims.
inputs: A shared Databricks program objective, such as governed lakehouse reporting and operational serving
Known delivery pressure, team constraints, and quality expectations
Agreement that tests and evidence matter more than optimistic status updates
outputs: A common vocabulary for phases, artifacts, and evidence
A decision to treat the spec as the contract for implementation
A baseline expectation that every claim can be verified
antipatterns: Starting notebook, job, or dashboard work before defining the expected behavior
Treating Superpowers as ceremony instead of a way to reduce rework
Reporting progress without evidence from tests, reviews, or verification
doneCriteria: The team can explain the full Fundamentals plus Phases 1-9 flow
Stakeholders agree that scope changes update the spec before code changes
The Databricks example has a clear business outcome and measurable quality bar

title: Phase 1 - Discovery
kicker: Find the real problem before solving it
goal: Capture stakeholders, business metrics, assumptions, constraints, risks, and success criteria for the Databricks program.
inputs: Stakeholder goals for bronze ingestion, silver standardization, gold marts, SQL analytics, and Lakebase serving
Existing data sources, freshness needs, governance constraints, and operational deadlines
Known risks such as schema drift, PII handling, lineage gaps, and cost ceilings
outputs: Discovery brief with business outcomes and non-goals
Risk and assumption log for data quality, governance, latency, and ownership
Measurable success criteria for the lakehouse workflow
antipatterns: Assuming every source should be ingested before ranking business value
Skipping governance discovery until after data lands in bronze
Leaving freshness, quality, or cost targets as vague aspirations
doneCriteria: Business metrics and stakeholder approvals are documented
Constraints and risks have owners or explicit follow-up decisions
The team knows which Databricks outcomes are in scope for the first release

title: Phase 2 - Specification
goal: Define architecture boundaries, data contracts, SLAs, governance rules, and expected behaviors before implementation starts.
inputs: Approved discovery brief and risk register
Source-to-bronze requirements, silver validation rules, gold mart definitions, and serving expectations
Security, catalog, lineage, retention, and PII requirements
outputs: Formal specification covering architecture boundaries and acceptance criteria
Data contracts for schemas, freshness, quality checks, and failure behavior
Governance and SLA rules that can be tested or reviewed
antipatterns: Writing implementation tasks from memory instead of from a reviewed spec
Specifying happy paths while leaving schema drift or late data undefined
Using Databricks features without defining why each one is needed
doneCriteria: Each requirement has observable behavior or evidence criteria
Non-goals and deferred decisions are explicit
Stakeholders can review the spec without reading implementation code

title: Phase 3 - Implementation Planning
kicker: Convert the spec into executable work
goal: Break the specification into ordered tasks across ingestion, modeling, governance, SQL serving, and operational serving.
inputs: Reviewed specification with acceptance criteria
Repository structure, team ownership, and release constraints
Dependency map for jobs, tables, tests, dashboards, and serving endpoints
outputs: Implementation plan with small testable tasks
Task ordering that separates independent tracks from blocking dependencies
Verification checkpoints tied back to specification requirements
antipatterns: Creating giant tasks such as build the lakehouse end to end
Planning UI, jobs, and governance changes without clear verification points
Ignoring dependency order between bronze ingestion, silver rules, and gold marts
doneCriteria: Every task has clear files, behavior, and test expectations
Parallelizable work is separated from sequential dependencies
The plan excludes later-scope artifact viewer, simulation, or comparison work unless explicitly assigned

title: Phase 4 - Environment and Workspace Setup
kicker: Protect the repo before changing it
goal: Demonstrate isolated Git workspaces and repository hygiene so implementation can proceed without trampling unrelated work.
inputs: Target branch, current git status, and known untracked or unrelated files
Local runtime requirements for static pages and Node tests
Decision about whether work should happen in the current branch or an isolated worktree
outputs: Clean understanding of the starting repository state
Workspace ready for scoped Task 3 edits only
Relative path strategy preserved for GitHub Pages deployment
antipatterns: Running destructive git commands to clean up work you did not create
Mixing phase content work with artifact viewer or simulation implementation
Assuming local absolute paths will work after GitHub Pages deployment
doneCriteria: Unrelated edits are identified and left untouched
All files to be changed are within the stated Task 3 scope
The page shell can load shared assets through relative URLs

title: Phase 5 - Test-Driven Development
goal: Write failing-first tests for quality, transformations, contracts, and serving expectations before implementation.
inputs: Implementation task with desired behavior and acceptance criteria
Existing test commands and project conventions
A minimal failing assertion that proves the missing behavior
outputs: A RED test that fails for the expected reason
Minimal implementation that turns the test GREEN
Regression coverage for the phase data and renderer contract
antipatterns: Writing Databricks transformation code first and adding tests after
Accepting a test error as a valid RED failure
Broadening scope while trying to make one focused test pass
doneCriteria: The failing test was observed before implementation
The test passes after the smallest useful implementation
The test validates behavior rather than incidental implementation details

title: Phase 6 - Systematic Debugging
goal: Apply root-cause diagnosis for failures in data quality, permissions, lineage, cost/performance, and orchestration.
inputs: A reproducible failure from tests, jobs, quality checks, or serving behavior
Observed evidence such as assertion output, job logs, query plans, or permission errors
The relevant spec requirement that should have been satisfied
outputs: Failure statement that separates symptoms from likely causes
Minimal reproduction or focused test that catches the issue
Fix verified against the original evidence
antipatterns: Changing cluster settings, SQL, and code at the same time without isolating cause
Treating a rerun success as proof that a flaky pipeline is fixed
Debugging from guesses while ignoring the spec and test output
doneCriteria: The root cause is explained with evidence
The fix is covered by a test or repeatable verification command
No unrelated behavior was changed while debugging

title: Phase 7 - Code Review
kicker: Use review to enforce the contract
goal: Use requesting and receiving review workflows to enforce correctness, maintainability, and spec alignment.
inputs: Scoped diff, tests, and implementation plan requirements
Spec sections that define expected Databricks behavior
Known risks such as governance gaps, brittle paths, or untested transformations
outputs: Review request that explains intent, scope, and verification evidence
Findings prioritized by correctness, regression risk, and missing tests
Disposition for each review item before merge
antipatterns: Reviewing for style while missing spec violations
Accepting unclear feedback without checking whether it is technically valid
Expanding the PR to include artifact viewer or simulation work during review
doneCriteria: Reviewers can map changes back to the implementation plan
High-severity findings are fixed or explicitly deferred with rationale
Tests and manual evidence are included with the review context

title: Phase 8 - Verification
goal: Validate end-to-end behavior against documented SLAs, governance requirements, and test evidence.
inputs: Specification acceptance criteria and implementation plan checklist
Fresh test output, rendered page checks, and review dispositions
Known deployment constraints such as GitHub Pages relative paths
outputs: Verification report with commands, results, and residual risks
Evidence that phase pages load structured JSON content correctly
Decision on whether the work is ready to commit or needs follow-up
antipatterns: Saying tests should pass without running them freshly
Using one passing unit test as proof that all navigation still works
Ignoring warnings or unrelated failures in verification output
doneCriteria: Required commands pass with current output
Every Task 3 requirement has been checked directly
Any remaining concern is reported explicitly instead of hidden

title: Phase 9 - Completion
kicker: Package the work without loose ends
goal: Finalize branch strategy, PR readiness, release notes, and environment cleanup after verification.
inputs: Passing verification evidence and reviewed diff
Commit message and exact list of changed files
Awareness of unrelated untracked or modified files that should remain untouched
outputs: Scoped commit for the completed task
Status summary with failing-then-passing test evidence
Clear next-step options for PR, merge, or continued task work
antipatterns: Committing unrelated plan files or local artifacts
Marking work complete before tests are rerun after final edits
Leaving the team unclear about what changed and what did not
doneCriteria: The commit contains only Task 3 files
The requested status, test summary, commit hash, and files changed are reported
No later tasks are implemented by accident
